title: Python Programming: An Introduction to Computer Science
author: John Zelle
description: My first programming book! It really laid down the basics for me, covering things like variables, conditional statements, and functions. Plus, there were these cool little projects to follow along, which made learning fun. Python was technically my second language after JavaScript, but it was the language that really taught my how to program.

title: Automate the Boring Stuff with Python
author: Al Sweigart
description: An absolute game changer. While the first book I read on Python introduced me to the conceptual and abstract ideas of CS, this second book helped me understand how to better put that knowledge into practice. After I finished reading it, I was better equipped to automate some previously manual tasks back at my job and helped me become more creative in figuring out ways to automate things not covered in the book.

title: How Computers Work
author: Matthew R Baker
description: Straight forward and to the point. The first part of the book is what I was most interested in. Being a completely non-technical person a few years ago, this book helped me understand computers a little bit better. The rest of the 2/3rds of the book are also useful to learn a little bit more about maintenance and solving computer issues.

title: Networking Made Easy
author: James Bernstein
description: A good intro to computer networking. It covers some basic concepts like networking protocols, IP addressing, and the internet. I use it as refresher whenever I need it. If you're a complete novice to networking, I highly recommend this one.

title: SQL: The Ultimate Beginner's Guide to Learn SQL Programming Step-by-Step
author: Mark Reed
description: A decent book on SQL. If you're starting out learning it for the first time, there may be better options there, but this is still an okay option if you need to understand the syntax a little bit better. It covers additional topics like joins, unions, DB adminstration, creating SQL views, and a few other things.

title: HTTP: The Definitive Guide
author: David Gourley, Brian Totty, Marjorie Sayer, Anshu Aggarwal, Sailu Reddy
description: This book is amazing! I'd say this is one of the most, if not the most impactful books of my career that made me really understand HTTP better. I was always curious about how web apps worked especially because I was trying to automate functionality at work without using selenium, but had no idea how things were operating on the web apps and sites I was working with. Not to sound cheesy, but this book helped me understand a whole new side of programming and engineering. Highly recommend it if you're a beginner or need a reference to HTTP.

title: Ry's Git Tutorial
author: Ryan Hodson
description: Simple and informative. This is a great introduction to learning version control and Git. Granted, you won't probably use all of features in Git that are taught in this book, but you'll learn the most essential parts that you'll most likely use in projects and in the real world.

title: Head First Java: A Brain-Friendly Guide
author: Kathy Sierra, Bert Bates, Trisha Gee
description: My first introduction to the Head First series. I personally loved the structure and way the authors present the teachings. I was already learning Java on my own when I started reading this, but this catapulted me into learning more advanced features of the language. It also gave me a more robust understanding of OOP through Java's language features.

title: Effective Java
author: Joshua Bloch
description: Excellent book! As the name implies, you'll come off being a better Java developer. Some concepts taught in this book are not just applicable to Java! Some other languages may have a different philosophy or flavor of how to implement certain concepts like Generics, but this book will help you make better sense of it all. Overall, I feel that this book made me a better engineer. The parts that stand out the most in terms of being most impactful were on OOP, intefaces, generics, and concurrency.

title: The Pragmatic Programmer: Your Journey To Mastery
author: David Thomas, Andrew Hunt
description: Whether you're a beginner or tenured software engineer, this is one of those books where you don't want to pass from this life to the next without reading. Lots of gems in here, but if I could highlight one it's that you should never depend on your job to learn new tools and techniques. Always strive to learn on your own. Your job and school will always have certain tools and techniques in place for you when you're working and studying, but don't let that limit you from trying new things on your own time.

title: Data Engineering with Python
author: Paul Crickard
description: A good conceptual foundation into DE. Was interested to know more about the field and see common practices with Python. The title is slightly misleading in that it covers a lot of NiFi. Not a bad thing necessarily, but perhaps a change of title might make more sense. The examples were a bit outdated and I had to look up a bit of documentation to get through the examples. Tech changes fast though so it's to be expected sometimes.

title: Explain the Cloud Like I'm 10
author: Todd Hoff
description: Amazing explaination of what the "cloud" and cloud computing is. The author explains things in such simplicity without over-simplifying the concepts of the cloud. Really helped me understand what the cloud is all about. The author includes lots of nice visuals too to help visualize what the data centers look like that provide all sorts of cloud services.

title: Soft Skills
author: John Sonmez
description: An excellent book that covers everything from how to better market yourself as a software engineer, to fitness lifestyle advice, to financial advice, and to stoicism (I'm serious). The advice on how to market yourself better was the highlight for me from the entire book and a huge reason for why I decided to start building my own site. I highly recommend it.

title: A Common-Sense Guide to Data Structures and Algorithms
author: Jay Wengrow
description: Wengrow does an excellent job explaining a variety of data structures and algorithms in this book. It's very language agnostic, so he uses different of programming languages to explain different concepts which I found refreshing. Highly recommend it if you're a beginner programmer and need a gentle introduction into data structures and algorithms. I find that it also serves as a good reference.

title: Fundamentals of Software Architecture
author: Mark Richards, Neal Ford
description: A great overview of the different types of approaches on designing software architecture. The authors give great explanations on the pros and cons of differing architecture models and when it's best to choose one over the other. They highlight a lot of principles on architecture that I found to be very insightful. Highly recommend it.

title: Mastering API Architecture
author: James Gough, Daniel Bryant, Matthew Auburn
description: Excellent book on API architecture. The authors explain the design aspects of creating your own API whether in REST or RPC. One of the things that stand out for me from this book is the content on service meshes and API gateways. Very informative and a great reference when designing your own APIs.

title: Code: The Hidden Language of Computer Hardware and Software
author: Charles Petzold
description: One of my first books I read when I started trying to get into software engineering. It starts off with two best friends communicating through non-binary code and builds off from there to explain the development of the binary code today used in our machines. Petzold does a great job explaining some rather difficult concepts in a very easy and understandable way. If you have a strong curiosity to understand how computers operate and work, then I highly recommend this.

title: Machine Learning for Absolute Beginners
author: Oliver Theobald
description: Not my first book on ML, but definitely recommend it as a first for anyone new to the subject. You get a gentle introduction into what ML is, data clean up practices to better train ML models, and some ML algorithms like linear regression and k-nearest neighbors. Theobald also explains things like artificial neural networks. Great book and is one of 3 from a series that Theobald has written.

title: Observability Engineering
author: Charity Majors, Liz Fong-Jones, George Miranda
description: Excellent book on what observability actually is. The authors do an excellent job making an important distinction between what monitoring and observability is. You'll get a great introduction into how to utilize observability solutions efficiently. If you're working as a DevOps engineer, SRE, or as a cloud engineer, then this is a great book to add to your library.

title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
author: Martin Kleppmann
description: This an amazing book on designing data intensive applications. Gives a nice foundation on data, how to manage distributed data, and covers things like batch and stream processing. If you're interested in getting involved in data either from a Software Engineering or Data Engineering perspective, try this book out.

title: DevOps for Dummies
author: Emily Freeman
description: This is a gentle introduction into what DevOps is and what it's not. If you're interested in adopting DevOps practices and processes into your engineering teams, but have little to no understanding of what DevOps is, try this book out.

title: Continuous Delivery
author: Jez Humble, David Farley
description: An excellent and thorough book on how to design and implement a deployment pipeline. It covers some foundational concepts in DevOps like continuous integration and delivery. There's also a few chapters on version control, managing data, infrastructure and more.

title: The DevOps Handbook
author: Gene Kim (Author), Jez Humble, Patrick Debois, John Willis, Nicole Forsgren
description: Another one by Jez Humble. Covers everything from adding feedback loops via telemetry, CI/CD, security, and other DevOps-focused practices and processes. If you're looking to expand your knowledge on DevOps, this is a great resource.

title: Accelerate
author: Nicole Forsgren PhD, Jez Humble, Gene Kim
description: Annnnd another one by Jez Humble. This explains the science and research into the impact of what works and what doesn't in software delivery practices and processes. An excellent book backed up by 4 years worth of data.
